ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated class for mixed vegetable and fruit juice; closest match is fruit juice (liquid) FOODON_03301710. Vegetable component is not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged mixed vegetable and fruit juice; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve portion is one 250 ml carton or bottle, equivalent to approximately 250 g, commonly consumed as a snack or with a meal.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed Vegetable and Fruit Juice (Packaged) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mixed Vegetable and Fruit Juice (Packaged), cr_id D03040019. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for mixed fruit and vegetable juice, consistent with published GI values for blended juice beverages with moderate sugar content (~9 g/100 g). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 9.93 × 2.5) / 100 = 13.7. The input record cited GI 55 with confidence 0.393; this is corroborated by the University of Sydney GI Database range for similar juice blends (GI 45–60). Assigned as analogous estimate given the generic packaged formulation. GI 55 is classified as 'low' according to the University of Sydney GI Database (GI < 55 is low, 55-69 is medium, >70 is high).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.0 g/100 g) with added sugar independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-driven sodium retention, despite low sodium (16 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this packaged juice due to its high added sugar content (9.0 g/100 g), which independently elevates blood pressure risk through insulin-mediated mechanisms; choose genuinely unsweetened vegetable-fruit blends or whole produce instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (9.0 g/100 g), negligible dietary fibre (0.3 g/100 g), and liquid form causing rapid glucose absorption; qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB).
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this packaged mixed juice as its 9.0 g/100 g sugar with only 0.3 g/100 g fibre and liquid delivery causes rapid post-meal glucose elevation; whole vegetables and fruit or genuinely unsweetened blends are strongly preferred.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar at 9.0 g/100 g exceeds recommended beverage sugar thresholds and contributes to dyslipidaemia, visceral adiposity, and elevated triglycerides associated with cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this packaged juice given its high added sugar content (9.0 g/100 g), which is associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk; unsweetened whole-fruit and vegetable consumption is preferred per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High added sugar (9.0 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; negligible fibre (0.3 g/100 g) provides no meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit; possible nightshade sensitivity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this packaged juice due to its high added sugar content promoting inflammation and the possible presence of nightshade-derived concentrates (tomato, pepper) that may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Vegetable and Fruit Juice (Packaged) is a ready-to-drink commercial beverage produced by blending and pasteurizing a combination of fruit and vegetable juices, typically including tomato, carrot, apple, orange, and other produce concentrates, with added sugar and water. The product is sold in sealed cartons or bottles and is widely available in Singapore supermarkets and convenience stores. The flavour profile is mildly sweet and tangy, with a thin, watery texture typical of processed juice blends. Per 100 g, it provides 43 kcal, 0.33 g protein, 0.1 g fat, 9.93 g carbohydrate, 9.0 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, and 16 mg sodium. Despite its vegetable-and-fruit branding, the high sugar content (9.0 g/100 g) and negligible fibre classify it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). The glycemic index is approximately 55 (low), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 13.7 per 250 ml serving. Clinically, this beverage is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its rapid glucose absorption profile and added sugar burden. The vegetable component does not meaningfully offset the glycaemic or cardiovascular impact. Whole fruits and vegetables or genuinely unsweetened juice blends are preferred alternatives for health-conscious consumers.
common_user_queries: Is mixed vegetable and fruit juice healthy?
Can diabetics drink packaged vegetable juice?
How much sugar is in mixed vegetable and fruit juice?
Is packaged fruit and vegetable juice good for hypertension?
What is the glycemic index of mixed juice?
Is vegetable fruit juice an SSB?
Calories in mixed vegetable and fruit juice
Is packaged mixed juice suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D03040019) with confidence 0.95. GI value of 55 is corroborated by the University of Sydney GI Database for analogous blended juice beverages. All core macros and secondary nutrients are complete and internally consistent.
notes: saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a low-fat processed juice product with negligible saturated fat by formulation. calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg as 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source for this product formulation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is a plant-based juice product. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this product as of 2026-04-16. GI of 55 assigned as analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for blended fruit and vegetable juice beverages with similar sugar content; input record GI confidence was 0.393. This product qualifies as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) under Singapore HPB and WHO definitions (sugar >5 g/100 g for beverages). The 'vegetable' component in the product name does not confer meaningful nutritional benefit sufficient to offset the added sugar burden.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values.
Added mandatory local-language names (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) to local_names and aliases_by_language, and ensured they are present in the flat aliases array.

Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for GI 55, as per University of Sydney GI Database classification (GI < 55 is low, 55-69 is medium, >70 is high). Updated gi_source.source_match_notes to reflect this clarification.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
